Node.js Basics, Part 1: Install It, Meet the V8 Engine, and Run Hello World
Here's another kickoff to a basics series. These are notes I'm writing mostly to get my own understanding of Node.js concepts and development a bit clearer. No long preamble — let's just get into it.
Whenever you're learning something new, the first thing to do is a bit of background reading.
No matter which language you're picking up, you usually go look up what it can do, where it's heading, that kind of thing. Node.js is no different.

The thing is, frontend and backend engineers traditionally learned different languages. For frontend folks, the main skills were JavaScript, CSS, and HTML, while the backend had plenty of options — C#, PHP, Python, and so on. But ever since Node.js showed up, frontend engineers can reach into the backend too, and the code you write there is mostly still JavaScript.
Node.js is built on top of the V8 engine, which Google put out.
So what does the V8 engine actually do? In short, it's the thing that turns your JavaScript into instructions the computer can understand — and Google Chrome ships with this same engine.
Every browser has its own engine, of course. Firefox, for example, uses Gecko and SpiderMonkey. I won't go deep on those here, since the focus is Node.js.
So what does Node.js have going for it?
It can run as a standalone server, without needing Apache HTTP Server or IIS
Lots of frameworks — Express.js, Socket.IO, Connect, and the newer Koa.js
You can develop in alternative languages (CoffeeScript, TypeScript, Dart, and so on)
Okay, enough talk — let's get the environment set up.
First, head to the official site to install Node.js.
Just go to the site and download it.
I'd recommend grabbing the LTS version (the long-term support release). If you go with Current (the cutting-edge release), you might run into packages that don't support it yet.
Install the LTS version — the long-term support release

Kicking off another basics series — this one's a set of notes I'm writing to get my own head straight on Node.js concepts and development. In this first post we'll cover what Node.js actually is, the V8 engine behind it, why it's worth using, how to install it, and how to run your very first Hello World.
